title: Project Plan (PDF extracted to JSON)

pages.text:
Modeling Diet-Driven Microbiome and Neurotransmitter Pathways 
Influencing Cognitive Performance 
Yana Evteeva 
McNair Academic High School 
Maria Nolau 
01 February 2026

Rationale‚Äã
‚Äã
The gut‚Äìbrain axis is a bidirectional biological system connecting the gastrointestinal 
tract and the central nervous system through neural, immune, endocrine, and metabolic signaling 
pathways. This system has become a major focus of research across neuroscience, nutrition, 
microbiology, and computational biology because it provides a mechanistic explanation for how 
diet can influence brain function. Cognitive processes such as attention, memory, learning, 
executive control, and stress regulation depend not only on neural circuitry but also on 
neurochemical balance and inflammatory state, both of which are strongly influenced by gut 
microbial metabolism.‚Äã
‚Äã
The human gut contains trillions of microorganisms collectively known as the gut 
microbiome. These microbes contribute to host physiology by fermenting dietary substrates, 
producing short-chain fatty acids such as acetate, propionate, and butyrate, synthesizing 
vitamins, regulating immune responses, and influencing the integrity of the intestinal and 
blood‚Äìbrain barriers. Microbial metabolites can enter systemic circulation and affect 
neuroinflammation, microglial activity, and neurotrophic signaling. Short-chain fatty acids in 
particular represent one of the strongest biochemical links between diet and cognition because 
they influence inflammatory tone, gut barrier permeability, and signaling pathways involved in 
serotonin production and stress regulation. In addition, certain gut bacteria influence neuroactive 
chemistry either directly through microbial metabolism or indirectly by modulating host 
neurotransmitter synthesis, including pathways related to serotonin, dopamine, and 
gamma-aminobutyric acid. These mechanisms form a biologically coherent chain in which diet 
shapes microbial ecology, microbial ecology shapes metabolic and neurochemical output, and 
those outputs influence cognitive performance.‚Äã

‚Äã
Despite this growing body of evidence, most gut‚Äìbrain studies remain correlational. 
Many studies demonstrate that healthier diets are associated with improved cognitive outcomes, 
but they do not explain the biological pathways responsible for these effects. Diet consists of 
complex nutrient combinations, the microbiome is highly individualized, and 
neurotransmitter-related outcomes depend on interactions between microbial metabolism, host 
physiology, and inflammation. Experimental studies that measure all of these layers 
simultaneously are difficult, expensive, and often impractical in human populations, particularly 
in adolescents. These limitations make it challenging to move beyond association toward 
mechanistic understanding.‚Äã
‚Äã
Computational modeling and artificial intelligence provide a powerful approach to 
address this challenge. Large public datasets exist that independently describe dietary intake, gut 
microbiome composition, metabolomic profiles, and health-related outcomes. However, these 
datasets are rarely integrated into a unified biological framework. This project uses a multi-stage 
machine learning approach to explicitly model the biological sequence linking diet to cognition. 
Instead of treating diet and cognition as directly connected variables, the model represents 
intermediate biological steps, including microbiome composition and metabolite production. 
This approach allows for simulation of how changes in diet propagate through biological systems 
and influence cognitive outcomes.‚Äã
‚Äã
This project builds directly on my prior research, which identified a strong correlation 
between diet quality and cognitive performance in high school students. The primary limitation 
of that study was the absence of biological measurements, which prevented identification of 
causal mechanisms. The current project addresses this limitation by modeling intermediate 
biological layers using public datasets, then validating predictions using previously collected

human cognitive performance data. This design maintains real-world relevance while adding 
biological interpretability.‚Äã
‚Äã
This research is important both scientifically and socially. Scientifically, it advances 
understanding of how specific nutrient patterns influence brain function through microbial and 
metabolic pathways. Socially, adolescent diets often contain high levels of ultra-processed foods, 
added sugars, and low fiber intake, dietary patterns associated with inflammation and metabolic 
dysregulation. If this model can identify nutrient and microbial signatures that predict improved 
attention and memory, the results could inform school nutrition policies, public health education, 
and future personalized nutrition strategies aimed at improving learning outcomes. 
Research Question‚Äã
How do diet-induced changes in gut microbiome composition and neurotransmitter-related 
metabolite production predict cognitive performance outcomes?‚Äã
‚Äã
Hypothesis‚Äã
Nutrient-dense dietary patterns, particularly higher fiber intake and higher omega-3 or 
polyunsaturated fat intake with lower added sugar intake, will predict microbiome profiles 
enriched in beneficial genera associated with short-chain fatty acid production and neuroactive 
metabolite pathways. These predicted metabolite profiles will be associated with improved 
cognitive performance outcomes, especially attention and memory, compared to dietary patterns 
high in refined sugars and saturated fats.‚Äã
‚Äã
Expected Outcomes‚Äã
Based on existing gut‚Äìbrain research, the integrated modeling system is expected to show that

higher fiber intake and greater plant diversity predict increased abundance of short-chain fatty 
acid‚Äìassociated microbial genera and higher predicted short-chain fatty acid production. 
Microbial profiles enriched in short-chain fatty acid‚Äìassociated and neuroactive genera are 
expected to predict higher levels of metabolites involved in neurotransmitter-related pathways, 
including metabolites linked to serotonin, dopamine, and gamma-aminobutyric acid signaling. 
The full diet to microbiome to metabolite modeling pipeline is expected to significantly improve 
prediction of cognitive outcomes compared to diet-only models, particularly for attention and 
memory measures.‚Äã
‚Äã
Materials‚Äã
This project is computational and does not involve laboratory materials. 
‚óè‚Äã Hardware and Computing Resources 
‚óã‚Äã Computer with reliable internet access 
‚óã‚Äã Local storage capacity sufficient for large public datasets 
‚óè‚Äã Software and Programming Environment 
‚óã‚Äã Python programming language for data preprocessing, modeling, and analysis 
‚óã‚Äã Jupyter Notebook or equivalent interactive environment to ensure reproducibility 
and documentation of analysis steps 
‚óè‚Äã Python Libraries and Tools 
‚óã‚Äã pandas and numpy for data cleaning, manipulation, and numerical operations 
‚óã‚Äã scikit-learn for supervised learning, model evaluation, and cross-validation 
‚óã‚Äã xgboost for ensemble-based regression modeling and performance benchmarking 
‚óã‚Äã matplotlib for data visualization

‚óã‚Äã shap for explainable AI analysis and feature importance interpretation 
‚óè‚Äã Publicly Available Datasets 
‚óã‚Äã NHANES dietary intake datasets for nutrient intake variables 
‚óã‚Äã USDA Food and Nutrient Database for nutrient definition standardization and 
nutrient mapping 
‚óã‚Äã Human Microbiome Project datasets for microbial abundance profiles 
‚óã‚Äã American Gut Project datasets for additional microbiome variability and 
validation 
‚óã‚Äã Metabolomics Workbench datasets for metabolite concentrations related to 
gut‚Äìbrain pathways 
‚óã‚Äã MetaboLights datasets for additional metabolomics data when available 
‚óè‚Äã Validation Dataset 
‚óã‚Äã De-identified 2025 student dataset containing only numeric diet quality scores and 
cognitive performance metrics, used solely for validation and benchmarking and 
not for model training 
Procedure 
1.‚Äã Download dietary intake data from NHANES and extract nutrient intake variables 
relevant to gut‚Äìbrain pathways, including fiber, total sugar, added sugar when available, 
saturated fat, polyunsaturated fat or omega-3 proxies, and selected micronutrients. 
2.‚Äã Use the USDA Food and Nutrient Database to standardize nutrient definitions and ensure 
consistency across dietary datasets.

3.‚Äã Clean dietary data by handling missing values using consistent criteria and normalizing 
nutrient variables when appropriate to allow comparison across individuals. 
4.‚Äã Download microbiome abundance profiles from public microbiome datasets and extract 
genus-level taxonomic tables. 
5.‚Äã Convert microbiome data to relative abundance and apply transformations when 
necessary to address compositional constraints. 
6.‚Äã Filter microbiome data to remove inconsistently measured or low-prevalence taxa while 
retaining biologically relevant genera associated with short-chain fatty acid production, 
neuroactive pathways, and inflammation. 
7.‚Äã Download metabolomics datasets that include short-chain fatty acids and 
neurotransmitter-related metabolites relevant to gut‚Äìbrain mechanisms. 
8.‚Äã Harmonize metabolite names across datasets, standardize concentration units, and handle 
missing values using appropriate filtering or imputation methods. 
9.‚Äã Retain metabolites relevant to gut‚Äìbrain pathways, focusing on short-chain fatty acids 
and metabolites involved in neurotransmitter synthesis or regulation. 
10.‚ÄãPrepare the 2025 student dataset by retaining only de-identified, numeric summary 
variables for diet quality and cognitive performance to be used solely for validation. 
11.‚ÄãTrain a supervised learning model to predict microbiome genus-level abundance patterns 
using dietary nutrient variables as inputs. 
12.‚ÄãEvaluate the nutrition-to-microbiome model using cross-validation and regression 
metrics, and identify the most influential nutrient features using explainable AI methods. 
13.‚ÄãTrain a second supervised learning model to predict metabolite concentrations using 
microbiome composition features as inputs.

14.‚ÄãEvaluate the microbiome-to-metabolite model using cross-validation and, when possible, 
cross-dataset testing to assess generalizability. 
15.‚ÄãTrain a third supervised learning model to predict cognitive performance outcomes using 
predicted metabolite profiles as inputs. 
16.‚ÄãValidate cognitive outcome predictions by comparing model outputs to observed 
cognitive performance metrics from the de-identified 2025 student dataset. 
17.‚ÄãIntegrate the three trained models into a single end-to-end pipeline representing the 
biological sequence from diet to microbiome to metabolite output to cognition. 
18.‚ÄãConduct simulation analyses by systematically modifying dietary input variables, such as 
increasing fiber intake or decreasing added sugar intake, and recording predicted changes 
in microbiome composition, metabolite profiles, and cognitive performance.‚Äã
 
Risk and Safety‚Äã
This project does not involve wet laboratory procedures, vertebrate animals, hazardous 
chemicals, or biological agents. No new human participants are recruited. All training datasets 
are publicly available and fully de-identified. The only human-related data used consist of 
de-identified summary metrics collected previously. There are no physical, biological, or 
chemical risks associated with this project. 
Data Analysis‚Äã
Data analysis includes descriptive statistics, correlation analysis, supervised learning evaluation, 
and explainable AI methods. Model performance is assessed using cross-validation and metrics 
including R¬≤, mean absolute error, and root mean squared error. Explainable AI tools such as 
SHAP values and permutation importance are used to identify key nutrients, microbial taxa, and

metabolite features driving predictions. Baseline diet-only cognitive prediction models are 
compared to the full pipeline to evaluate the contribution of intermediate biological modeling. 
Bibliography  
Psychology Today. ‚ÄúYour 'Healthy' Diet Could Be Quietly Killing Your Brain.‚Äù Psychology 
Today, 2019, 
https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/blog/the-optimalist/201310/your-healthy-diet-could-be-qui
etly-killing-your-brain 
 
Nature Reviews Neuroscience. "Effects of Diet on Brain Function and Cognition." Nature 
https://www.nature.com/articles/s41538-017-0008-y 
 
Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health. "Mediterranean Diet and Cognitive Function." 
Harvard https://hsph.harvard.edu/research/health-professionals/ 
 
National Institute on Aging. "What Do We Know About Diet and Prevention of Alzheimer‚Äôs 
Disease" NIA 
https://www.nia.nih.gov/health/alzheimers-and-dementia/what-do-we-know-about-diet-and-prev
ention-alzheimers-disease 
 
Harvard Health Publishing "Probiotics may help boost mood and cognitive function" Harvard 
https://www.health.harvard.edu/mind-and-mood/probiotics-may-help-boost-mood-and-cognitive-
function

ts/#:~:text=The%20first%20study%20found%20that,Between%20Omega%2D3%20and%20Hea
dache%3F

Screening Mild Cognitive Impairment in Community Dwellers‚Äù Oxford

m=fulltext

https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-019-1238-8.

786‚Äì796, 
https://www.researchgate.net/publication/301277603_Gut_microbiome_remodeling_induces_de
pressive-like_behaviors_through_a_pathway_mediated_by_the_host's_metabolism
